turns.content: OK OK OK I have NEWS and I need to talk strategy immediately!!!
I can feel the energy through the screen! What's the news?
I GOT A SLOT AT CANADIAN MUSIC WEEK!!! March 15th!! like THE Canadian Music Week!! I'm doing a showcase set and there are going to be A&Rs in the building fam this could change EVERYTHING for me

Canadian Music Week is a HUGE opportunity — congratulations! That's one of the biggest industry events in the country. Having A&Rs, label reps, and booking agents in the room during your set is exactly the kind of exposure that can accelerate a career overnight.

March 15th — that's only 5 days away. So we need to be strategic and focused. First things first:

- How long is your set?
- Do you know what venue/stage you're on?
- Do you know who else is on the lineup around your time slot?

30 minutes!! which is short but honestly for a showcase that's standard right?? I don't know who else is on the lineup yet they're still finalizing. and the venue I'm not 100% sure yet either they said they'll confirm by Wednesday

30 minutes is completely standard for a CMW showcase. Every artist gets a short window — it's designed that way so industry people can see multiple acts in one night. The challenge is making every single minute count.

30 minutes means you need to think of this less like a DJ set and more like a concentrated statement of who you are as an artist. Here's the framework:

**The 30-Minute Showcase Formula:**
- **Minutes 1-5**: Open strong. No slow build — you don't have time. Grab attention immediately. Something that makes people stop their conversations and look up
- **Minutes 5-15**: Show your range. This is where you demonstrate what makes you different — your dancehall roots, your bass music production, your sound design
- **Minutes 15-25**: Peak section. Your absolute best material. If an A&R is only paying attention for 5 minutes, make it these 5 minutes. Play your own productions here
- **Minutes 25-30**: Close with impact. Leave a lasting impression. Something memorable they'll be talking about after

The biggest mistake in a showcase set is trying to do too much. What's the ONE thing you want people to walk away thinking about you?

I want them to walk away thinking 'that girl has a SOUND nobody else has.' like I want my dancehall-bass-experimental ting to hit them different. I need them to hear something they haven't heard before ahlie

Perfect. That's a clear goal and it should drive every track selection decision.

For the track list, here's how I'd approach it:

1. **Lead with your own production** — opening with an original track immediately tells the room "this person makes music, they're not just playing other people's tracks." Pick something with instant energy and a signature sound
2. **Curate ruthlessly** — in 30 minutes you'll play maybe 8-10 tracks max. Every single one needs to justify its spot. Ask yourself: does this track show something about me that the others don't?
3. **Feature 3-4 of your own productions** throughout the set. That's the proof of concept for any A&R — they want to hear YOUR music
4. **Use transitions as flex moments** — how you blend tracks is part of your artistry. A creative transition between genres shows skill and vision

Do you have enough original productions ready that you feel confident showcasing?

I've got like 6 tracks that are fully finished and mixed properly. some of them have been KILLING at my regular Friday sets so I know they work on a dancefloor. but a showcase crowd is different from a club crowd right??

Yes, very different dynamic. Here's the key difference:

**Club crowd**: They're there to dance and have a good time. They respond to energy, drops, and groove. They're facing the dancefloor.

**Showcase crowd**: A mix of industry people actively evaluating you and regular attendees. The industry people are often standing near the back or sides, watching. They're listening to your production quality, your originality, your stage presence. They're thinking about commercial potential and artistic identity.

So your approach needs to split the difference:
- The tracks still need to HIT — a dead room doesn't impress anyone, industry or not
- But the production details matter more. Clear, well-mixed tracks with interesting sound design will catch an A&R's ear even at lower volumes
- Stage presence matters — how you carry yourself behind the decks, your energy, your confidence

Those 6 finished tracks that work on dancefloors are your foundation. Pick the 3-4 strongest and build the set around them. Which ones get the biggest reaction at your Friday sets?

there's this one track called 'Duppy Frequency' that literally STOPS the room every time I play it. big bassline with this eerie dancehall vocal chop over it. and another one called 'Bruk Out Protocol' that's more high energy — always gets the biggest forward from the crowd

Those sound like your anchors. Here's how I'd place them:

- **'Duppy Frequency'** with the eerie vocal chop and big bassline — that's your OPENER. If it stops the room at a club, imagine what it does in a showcase where people aren't expecting it. An A&R hears that in the first 30 seconds and they're paying attention for the rest of your set
- **'Bruk Out Protocol'** is your peak-section track. Place it around the 18-22 minute mark when you want maximum energy. That's the climax of your showcase story

So the arc becomes:
1. Open with 'Duppy Frequency' — immediate statement, this is who I am
2. Build through your curated selection — show range, show taste
3. Climax with 'Bruk Out Protocol' — prove you can move a room
4. Close with something that lingers — maybe a more atmospheric original that shows your depth

Do you have something more atmospheric or melodic in your catalog for that closing slot? You want the last thing they hear to be different from the high-energy tracks — something that shows you're not one-dimensional.

I do actually!! I've got this ting called 'Grandma's Veranda' — it's like a dub-influenced ambient piece with field recordings from Jamaica. rainfall, crickets, a radio playing in the background. it's beautiful but I wasn't sure if it was too chill for a showcase

No, that's EXACTLY what should close your set. Think about it — after 25 minutes of proving you can bring energy, hard production, and dancefloor heat, you end with something deeply personal and completely unexpected. 'Grandma's Veranda' with field recordings from Jamaica?

That does several things:
- It shows artistic depth and range — you're not just a bass music DJ, you're a storyteller
- It creates an emotional contrast that makes the whole set more memorable
- The title and concept alone tell a story about your roots and identity
- It gives the room a moment to breathe and THINK about what they just experienced

An A&R hearing your set go from 'Duppy Frequency' to 'Bruk Out Protocol' to 'Grandma's Veranda' is hearing a fully formed artist, not just someone who makes bangers.

Your setlist is basically writing itself:
1. 'Duppy Frequency' — statement
2. Curated selection — journey
3. 'Bruk Out Protocol' — climax
4. 'Grandma's Veranda' — emotional close

That's a 30-minute story with a beginning, middle, and end.

I literally have goosebumps right now. when you lay it out like that it makes so much sense!! it's like a whole narrative. ok what about the practical stuff — should I bring business cards?? what do I do if an A&R actually approaches me after??

Yes to being prepared for those conversations. Here's your post-set game plan:

**What to have ready:**
- Business cards or a simple card with your name, Instagram, email, and a QR code linking to your music. Keep it clean and memorable
- Your phone charged and ready — people will want to exchange contacts on the spot
- A private SoundCloud or Google Drive link with your 3-4 best tracks in high quality, ready to send immediately when someone asks "can you send me something?"

**If an A&R approaches you:**
- Be yourself. Don't pitch, don't oversell. Your set already did the talking
- Listen to what THEY say first. Let them tell you what caught their attention
- Ask questions back: What kind of artists are you looking for? What projects are you working on?
- Get their contact and follow up within 48 hours — not the same night, not a week later
- Never agree to anything on the spot. "That sounds amazing, let me think about it" is always the right answer to any offer made at a showcase

**Common A&R approaches:**
- "I really liked your set" — say thank you, ask what specifically caught their ear
- "Have you got any music you can send?" — this is the big one. Send that private link the NEXT DAY with a short, professional message
- "Are you signed?" — be honest about where you're at. Independent and hungry is not a weakness, it's an opportunity for them

Do you have all those materials ready, or do you need to put them together this week?

I need to put the link together for sure. I've got a SoundCloud but I should make a private playlist with just the BEST tings right?? and I don't have business cards I need to get those printed ASAP. 5 days to go ahhhh!!!

Five days is tight but totally doable. Here's your prep checklist day by day:

**Today (Monday):**
- Create a private SoundCloud playlist or Google Drive folder with your 4-5 best tracks in WAV or 320kbps MP3
- Order business cards online — lots of services do next-day or 2-day delivery. Keep the design simple: your name, "DJ / Producer," email, Instagram handle, and a QR code to your music

**Tuesday-Wednesday:**
- Finalize your setlist. Practice the full 30 minutes start to finish at least twice. Time it precisely — going over your slot at a showcase is a bad look
- Confirm venue details when they send them

**Thursday:**
- Final practice run. Record it and listen back critically
- Prep your USB drives — bring at least TWO as backup
- Pick your outfit. You want to look like you belong on that stage

**Friday (March 15):**
- Arrive early. Check the sound system, meet the sound engineer, test your setup
- Bring water, stay calm, and trust your preparation

The most important thing: you got this slot because someone believed in your music. The hard part is done — now you just have to show up and be yourself. What's your biggest worry right now?

honestly?? my biggest worry is that I'll get too in my head and not bring the energy I bring at my regular sets. like what if the pressure gets to me and I choke?? this is the biggest stage I've ever had

That fear is completely normal and honestly it's a good sign — it means you understand how big this is and you're taking it seriously.

Here's the reality check: you play for a demanding, music-literate crowd every single Friday. You've been battle-tested. A CMW showcase room is just another room with speakers and people. The decks are the same. The music is the same. YOU are the same.

Practical tips for managing nerves:

- **Pre-set routine**: Whatever you do before your Friday sets — same thing before CMW. Same warmup, same energy. Routine kills anxiety
- **First track confidence**: You already know 'Duppy Frequency' works. You've seen it stop rooms. Trusting that opener means you walk up to the decks knowing your first move is a guaranteed win. After that, momentum takes over
- **Focus on the music, not the room**: Once the first track drops, look at the decks, feel the bass, get into YOUR zone. You can't control who's watching. You can control how you play
- **Remember: they WANT you to be good.** A&Rs at showcases are hoping to discover someone. They're rooting for you. They're not there to judge — they're there to find their next signing

You've been building toward this moment. Trust the work you've put in.

ok you're right you're right. I've been doing this every week and KILLING it. CMW is just another room with speakers and people. I'm gonna write that on my mirror lol. ok I gotta go start prepping that private playlist and order business cards RIGHT NOW. this is happening!!! thank you fam for real this conversation just gave me the whole blueprint!!!

Go handle your business! Private playlist, business cards, practice runs — you've got your checklist. And that setlist arc from 'Duppy Frequency' to 'Grandma's Veranda' is going to turn heads.

March 15th is YOUR night. Walk in there knowing you earned that slot and you've got something nobody else in that room has. Go make it count! Good luck!
